content.text: The original Transformer from Vaswani et al. (2017) pairs two distinct stacks: an
that builds bidirectional contextual representations of the input sequence, and a
that generates output tokens autoregressively by attending to both prior generated tokens and the encoder's output through

. Most modern agent-facing LLMs are decoder-only, yet understanding the full encoder–decoder architecture is essential: cross-attention is the blueprint for every mechanism that lets a generative model consult external memory, retrieved context, or a separate modality while producing output.
An encoder–decoder Transformer processes a source sequence into a fixed-length set of contextual vectors (the encoder's output, also called
), then generates a target sequence step by step. At each decoding step the decoder queries the encoder memory with cross-attention to retrieve relevant information from the source.
— stacked layers each containing bidirectional self-attention and a feed-forward sub-layer; every token attends to every other token in the source sequence.
— stacked layers each containing masked (causal) self-attention, cross-attention over encoder outputs, and a feed-forward sub-layer; at inference only past generated tokens are visible.
come from the decoder's current hidden states; keys
come from the encoder output, which is computed once and reused at every decoding step.
— projects the decoder's top-layer vector to vocabulary logits and samples or greedily selects the next token.
Decoder-only GPT-style models dominate today's agent API landscape, yet the encoder–decoder split introduced three ideas that every practitioner of agentic systems must internalize:

The encoder's bidirectional attention builds the richest possible representation of source material; the decoder generates with full access to that representation at every step. Decoder-only models do both in one causal pass, which is faster at scale but fuses the roles.

RAG pipelines, multimodal models (vision encoder + text decoder), and tool-result injection all implement a form of cross-attention: a generative stack queries a fixed external representation. Understanding the original mechanism makes these patterns transparent.

Because the encoder is computed once per input, its representations can be cached, pre-indexed, or produced by a specialized model (e.g., a BERT-style reranker), enabling efficient two-stage retrieval + generation pipelines.
— each encoder token sees the full source context in both directions, producing richer representations than causal-only attention.
— encoder outputs are computed once per source; multiple decoder calls (e.g., beam search hypotheses or parallel generation) all share the same encoder KV pairs without recomputation.
— the architecture is naturally matched to tasks with a fixed source and variable-length target: translation, summarization, code transpilation, structured extraction.
— the encoder can be pre-trained independently (as in BERT) or replaced with a domain-specific model (e.g., a protein structure encoder paired with a text decoder).

— vision-language models (Flamingo, BLIP-2) use a vision encoder whose outputs feed into a text decoder via cross-attention, a direct application of the encoder–decoder pattern across modality boundaries.
— an encoder–decoder model of the same total parameter count as a decoder-only model devotes half the parameters to each task; neither half is as deep as a pure decoder of equal total size.

— the encoder runs once, then the decoder runs N times (one per generated token). For long outputs and short inputs the overhead is negligible; for many short generations from a fixed long source, the amortization is excellent. But serving infrastructure must host both stacks simultaneously.

— decoder-only models trained on vast next-token prediction corpora achieve better general language generation and instruction following. Encoder–decoder models excel on the seq2seq tasks they were fine-tuned for but rarely match GPT-scale models on free-form tasks.
— each decoder layer stores cross-attention KV pairs derived from the full encoder sequence. For a long source this multiplies the KV cache size by the number of decoder layers.

— the two stacks must be trained jointly with careful alignment; the encoder's representation space must be compatible with the decoder's cross-attention projections. Separate pre-training followed by fine-tuning can work but requires more care than a single autoregressive objective.
, the encoder maps each to an embedding plus positional encoding, then applies
— no causal mask is applied, so every source token attends to every other. The top-layer outputs
are the encoder memory used by the decoder.
, the decoder has access to the previously generated tokens
. Each decoder layer applies three sub-layers in order:
— attends over prior target tokens only (causal mask), building a contextual representation of what has been generated so far.
from the decoder's current state; keys and values
from encoder memory. The attention score
selects which source positions are most relevant to the current generation step.
— position-wise MLP that mixes cross-attended features nonlinearly.

Encoder layer:
  Input h → SelfAttn(h,h,h) → +Residual → LayerNorm → FFN → +Residual → LayerNorm → h'
  Attention: all n source tokens attend to all n source tokens (no mask)

Decoder layer:
  Input y → MaskedSelfAttn(y,y,y) → +Residual → LayerNorm
         → CrossAttn(Q=y, K=H_enc, V=H_enc) → +Residual → LayerNorm
         → FFN → +Residual → LayerNorm → y'
  MaskedSelfAttn: token t sees only tokens 1..t (causal mask)
  CrossAttn: token t can attend to all n encoder positions

Final step:
  y'_top → Linear(d_model → vocab_size) → Softmax → next token
During training the decoder receives the ground-truth target tokens as input (teacher forcing) and predicts the next token at every position simultaneously. The causal mask prevents position
from attending to future ground-truth tokens, so training and inference are consistent: position
in training sees exactly what position
in inference sees—previously generated (or fed) tokens only.
The diagram shows the two stacks side by side. The encoder (left, blue) processes the full source sequence with bidirectional self-attention and produces encoder memory

. The dashed green arrow carries the encoder's key–value pairs to every cross-attention sub-layer in the decoder (right, amber). The decoder generates the target sequence autoregressively through masked self-attention, cross-attention, and a final linear + softmax projection.

Choose an encoder–decoder model when your task fits the seq2seq mold: a fixed source sequence maps to a variable-length target sequence, and the two sequences have distinct vocabularies, lengths, or meaning.
— source and target languages have different vocabularies; bidirectional encoding of the source is beneficial.
— long document (source) compressed to a short summary (target); encoder–decoder handles the length asymmetry naturally.
— long context source (retrieved knowledge base) produces a short structured JSON answer; encoder compresses source, decoder generates structure.
— T5 and BART are strong starting points for tasks with labeled source–target pairs and limited compute budget for a full GPT-scale model.
— vision encoder + text decoder (BLIP-2, Flamingo) leverages cross-attention to ground generated text in image features.

Use a decoder-only model (GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA) when you need general instruction following or open-ended dialogue. Use encoder–decoder (T5, BART, FLAN-T5) when you have labeled seq2seq data and want to fine-tune a smaller specialized model.
Loading and running a T5 encoder–decoder model

# pip install transformers torch sentencepiece
from transformers import T5ForConditionalGeneration, T5Tokenizer
import torch

model_name = "t5-base"  # or "t5-small", "google/flan-t5-base"
tokenizer = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(model_name)
model.eval()

def run_enc_dec(source_text: str, max_new_tokens: int = 128) -> str:
    """Run a complete encoder-decoder forward pass and return decoded output."""
    # T5 uses a task prefix: "summarize:", "translate English to German:", etc.
    inputs = tokenizer(
        source_text,
        return_tensors="pt",
        truncation=True,
        max_length=512,
    )

    with torch.no_grad():
        # encoder runs once; decoder runs autoregressively
        output_ids = model.generate(
            **inputs,
            max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens,
            num_beams=4,
            early_stopping=True,
        )

    return tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True)

# Summarization
source = (
    "summarize: The encoder-decoder Transformer separates the "
    "reading and writing steps. The encoder processes the source "
    "with bidirectional attention, and the decoder generates the "
    "target using cross-attention over the encoder outputs."
)
print(run_enc_dec(source))
# -> "The encoder-decoder Transformer separates reading and writing."
Inspecting encoder memory and cross-attention weights

import torch
from transformers import T5ForConditionalGeneration, T5Tokenizer

tokenizer = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained("t5-small")
model = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained("t5-small")
model.eval()

source = "translate English to German: The encoder builds rich source representations."
target = "Der Encoder baut reichhaltige Quelldarstellungen auf."

src_enc = tokenizer(source, return_tensors="pt")
tgt_enc = tokenizer(target, return_tensors="pt").input_ids

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(
        **src_enc,
        labels=tgt_enc,
        output_attentions=True,
        output_hidden_states=True,
    )

# Encoder memory: last hidden state of the encoder
encoder_memory = outputs.encoder_last_hidden_state
print("Encoder memory shape:", encoder_memory.shape)
# -> torch.Size([1, n_src_tokens, d_model])

# Cross-attention weights from each decoder layer
for layer_idx, cross_attn in enumerate(outputs.cross_attentions):
    # cross_attn: (batch, heads, n_tgt_tokens, n_src_tokens)
    avg = cross_attn.mean(dim=1)  # average over heads
    print(f"Layer {layer_idx} cross-attn (tgt x src): {avg.shape}")
    # Find which source token each target token attends to most
    top_src = avg[0].argmax(dim=-1)
    print(f"  Top source idx per target token: {top_src.tolist()}")
Fine-tuning T5 for structured JSON extraction

# Demonstrates fine-tuning an encoder-decoder model for a structured output task.
# Requires: pip install transformers datasets torch
from transformers import (
    T5ForConditionalGeneration,
    T5Tokenizer,
    Seq2SeqTrainer,
    Seq2SeqTrainingArguments,
    DataCollatorForSeq2Seq,
)
from datasets import Dataset

tokenizer = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained("t5-small")
model = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained("t5-small")

# Toy dataset: natural language -> JSON extraction
raw_data = [
    {
        "source": "extract: John ordered 3 laptops at $999 each on 2024-01-15.",
        "target": '{"customer": "John", "qty": 3, "unit_price": 999, "date": "2024-01-15"}',
    },
    {
        "source": "extract: Alice bought 1 monitor for $349 on 2024-02-20.",
        "target": '{"customer": "Alice", "qty": 1, "unit_price": 349, "date": "2024-02-20"}',
    },
]

def tokenize(batch):
    model_inputs = tokenizer(
        batch["source"], max_length=128, truncation=True, padding="max_length"
    )
    labels = tokenizer(
        batch["target"], max_length=64, truncation=True, padding="max_length"
    )
    model_inputs["labels"] = labels["input_ids"]
    return model_inputs

dataset = Dataset.from_list(raw_data).map(tokenize, batched=True)

training_args = Seq2SeqTrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./t5-extract",
    num_train_epochs=5,
    per_device_train_batch_size=2,
    predict_with_generate=True,
    logging_steps=1,
    save_strategy="no",
)

trainer = Seq2SeqTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    data_collator=DataCollatorForSeq2Seq(tokenizer, model=model),
)
trainer.train()

# Inference after fine-tuning
test_input = "extract: Bob purchased 2 keyboards at $79 on 2024-03-01."
ids = tokenizer(test_input, return_tensors="pt").input_ids
out = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=64)
print(tokenizer.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# -> {"customer": "Bob", "qty": 2, "unit_price": 79, "date": "2024-03-01"}

— reframes every NLP task as seq2seq with task prefixes. Encoder–decoder architecture; pre-trained on C4 with a masked span denoising objective; fine-tuned on translation, summarization, QA, classification.
— encoder–decoder with BERT-style bidirectional encoder; denoising pre-training (shuffle, delete, mask); excels at summarization (CNN/DailyMail benchmark) and translation.
— T5 instruction-tuned on 1800+ tasks; strong few-shot performance on a small model footprint; popular for latency-sensitive deployment.
— vision encoder (frozen ViT) + frozen LLM (OPT or Flan-T5); a lightweight Q-Former bridging module uses cross-attention to extract image features relevant to text queries.
— audio encoder (Mel spectrogram → Transformer) plus a text decoder; cross-attention aligns generated text with audio frames; state-of-the-art speech recognition and translation.

A customer support automation pipeline at a large e-commerce company uses an encoder–decoder architecture to convert raw support tickets (sometimes 2 000+ words with order history, chat transcripts, and policy excerpts) into structured resolution actions:
— a BERT-based retriever (encoder-only) fetches the top-5 relevant policy paragraphs from a 50 000-document knowledge base.

— the ticket text plus retrieved paragraphs are concatenated and fed to the encoder of a fine-tuned FLAN-T5-XL. Bidirectional attention builds a representation that interleaves ticket context with policy language.
— the decoder generates a JSON action:
. Constrained decoding (token masking) enforces valid JSON at every step.
— 94% of routine tickets are resolved without human review; median latency 340 ms (encoder: 80 ms, decoder: 260 ms for 25 tokens).
— BERT and RoBERTa have no decoder and no autoregressive training objective; they cannot generate text. Adding a linear head does classification, not generation.
Forgetting to prepend task prefixes with T5
— T5 is conditioned on task prefixes like
. Omitting the prefix causes the model to default to a generic denoising mode and produce poor results.
— T5-base has a 512-token encoder limit. Truncating a 2 000-word document silently discards most of the content. Use T5-11B or a long-context variant for long inputs.
Applying causal mask to the encoder

— a common implementation error is reusing the same attention layer for encoder and decoder without toggling the mask. The encoder must be bidirectional; applying a causal mask to it degrades source representations significantly.
Decoding with greedy search for diverse outputs
— greedy decoding always picks the highest-probability token; it often collapses diverse summarizations to a single generic phrase. Use beam search or nucleus sampling for quality.
Ignoring exposure bias at fine-tune time

— teacher forcing feeds ground-truth tokens during training; at inference the decoder feeds its own (potentially wrong) predictions. This mismatch grows with output length. Scheduled sampling or RL-based fine-tuning (e.g., RLHF, REINFORCE) can mitigate it.
Start with FLAN-T5 for instruction-following tasks
— it covers hundreds of task types and often achieves strong zero-shot results without fine-tuning. Fine-tune only if zero-shot quality is insufficient.
— when the source is fixed (same document, multiple queries), run the encoder once and cache
. Each decoder call then only pays the decoder cost (typically 3–5x less compute).
Use constrained decoding for structured outputs
— libraries like Outlines or Guidance can mask invalid tokens at each step, guaranteeing syntactically valid JSON, SQL, or XML from the decoder without post-processing.
Monitor cross-attention entropy as a quality signal

— if cross-attention weights are nearly uniform across source tokens, the decoder is not grounding its outputs in the source. This often indicates a truncation issue, a domain mismatch, or a need for more fine-tuning.
Evaluate with ROUGE and BERTScore together

— ROUGE measures n-gram overlap (good for faithfulness); BERTScore measures semantic similarity using contextual embeddings (better for paraphrase). Both together give a rounder picture of summarization and translation quality.
Encoder–decoder models have two distinct cost profiles that compound when serving at scale.
Excellent if reused across many decoder calls
Grows with output length t and source length n
Fixed once encoder runs; reused at every decoding step
Memory per request grows with L and n
— run one encoder forward pass and tile the encoder memory for a batch of decoder calls (beam search, diverse generation, or multiple downstream queries about the same source).
— halves memory; necessary for T5-11B on consumer GPUs. Use
Sliding window encoding for long documents

— split the source into overlapping chunks, encode each independently, concatenate encoder outputs, and feed the combined memory to the decoder. Alternatively, use Longformer-Encoder-Decoder (LED) which has sparse attention in the encoder.
T5, BART, FLAN-T5 out-of-the-box; Seq2SeqTrainer for fine-tuning
WMT translation, CNN/DM summarization, XSUM all available
Use rouge_score or sacrebleu for translation
JSON schema enforcement at decode time; supports T5 and BART
Whisper and T5 support; INT8 quantization; used in OpenAI's whisper.cpp port
Causal self-attn only; prompt fused with generation

In agentic pipelines the three architectures are often combined: an encoder-only retriever selects documents, a decoder-only LLM reasons and plans, and an encoder–decoder fine-tuned model handles structured output tasks with deterministic formatting constraints.

— if user-controlled text is passed as the encoder source without sanitization, an adversary can inject instructions that override the intended task prefix. Separate trusted and untrusted input segments and validate output schema.

— encoder–decoder models can generate plausible-sounding facts not present in the source. Always include faithfulness evaluation (e.g., NLI-based entailment check) before deploying summarization outputs in high-stakes contexts.
— silently truncating the encoder input can cause critical information (e.g., a legal clause or a numeric field) to be dropped. Log encoder input lengths and alert when truncation occurs.
— T5 and BART checkpoints differ across Hugging Face revisions. Pin model revisions with
to guarantee deterministic output in production.
— beam search with small beam width (2–4) can produce nearly identical candidates that all fail in the same way. For reliability-critical tasks, use diverse beam search (
) or top-p sampling with a validation step.
The encoder builds a bidirectional representation of the full source sequence using unrestricted self-attention.
The decoder generates the target sequence autoregressively using masked self-attention (sees only past generated tokens) plus cross-attention (queries encoder memory at every step).
from encoder — is the foundational blueprint for every mechanism that grounds a generative model in external information (RAG context, visual features, tool results).
Encoder–decoder models (T5, BART, FLAN-T5) are the go-to choice for well-defined seq2seq tasks with labeled data; decoder-only models dominate open-ended generation and agent APIs.
Encoder memory is computed once and reused across all decoder steps and beam hypotheses — a key performance lever when serving many generations from a fixed source.
Modern agentic systems often use all three architecture types together: encoder-only for retrieval/reranking, decoder-only for planning, and encoder–decoder for structured output generation.
Think of the encoder as a
who reads the source text in full and marks every passage with contextual highlights. The decoder is a

who, at each word, glances at the reader's highlighted notes (cross-attention) to decide what to say next. Decoder-only models are a writer who has already memorized the source alongside everything else they know — faster, but with less explicit structure separating understanding from generation.
